grammar_topics.title: Past Tense Introduction
grammar_topics.description: Introduction to basic past tense verbs
grammar_topics.rule: Past tense is formed by modifying verb roots with specific patterns

grammar_topics.title: Future with 'سوف' and 'ح'
grammar_topics.description: Expressing future tense using 'سوف' or the prefix 'ح'
grammar_topics.rule: Add 'سوف' or 'ح' before the present tense verb for future meaning

grammar_topics.title: Adjective Placement
grammar_topics.description: Understanding where adjectives appear in Arabic sentences
grammar_topics.rule: Adjectives usually come after nouns in Arabic, unlike English

grammar_topics.title: Negation with 'لا'
grammar_topics.description: Using the negative particle 'لا' (la) to negate verbs
grammar_topics.rule: Place 'لا' before the verb or adjective to negate

script.speaker: Person B
script.translation: My day was beautiful, thank you

script.speaker: Person B
script.translation: Bright and good morning to you

cultural_insights.title: Morning Greetings
cultural_insights.description: In the Arab world, 'صباح الخير' (Sabah al-khair - Good morning) and the response 'صباح النور' (Sabah an-noor - Good morning with light) are essential daily rituals.

cultural_insights.title: Concept of Time
cultural_insights.description: In many Arab cultures, time is viewed more flexibly than in Western cultures. Relationships and the present moment are often prioritized over strict scheduling.

cultural_insights.title: Weekly Rhythms
cultural_insights.description: The Islamic week emphasizes Friday (الجمعة) as the holy day. Many Arab countries structure their work weeks around this, with Friday and Saturday as weekends in some places.

cultural_insights.title: Seasonality
cultural_insights.description: Islamic lunar calendar (Hijri) determines religious holidays and seasons, which differ from the Gregorian calendar used in the West.

exercises.question: How do you say 'morning' in Arabic?
exercises.explanation: 'صباح' (sabah) means morning, the time after sunrise.

exercises.question: What is the correct past tense form of 'to write' (كتب) in first person?
exercises.explanation: 'كتبت' (katabtu) is the first person singular past tense 'I wrote'.

exercises.question: How do you express 'I will go' using سوف?
exercises.explanation: 'سوف أذهب' (sawfa adhhabu) means 'I will go' in the future tense.

exercises.question: Where does the adjective come in Arabic?
exercises.explanation: In Arabic, adjectives come after the noun they modify.

exercises.question: How do you negate a verb in Arabic?
exercises.explanation: The negative particle 'لا' (la) is placed before verbs or adjectives to negate them.

exercises.question: What is 'غدا' (tomorrow)?
exercises.explanation: 'غدا' (ghada) means tomorrow, the day after today.

exercises.question: Complete: 'لا ___' (I don't know)
exercises.explanation: 'لا أعرف' (la a'rif) means 'I don't know' with the negative particle 'لا'.

exercises.question: How do you say 'good evening' in Arabic?
exercises.explanation: 'مساء الخير' (Masaa al-khair) means 'good evening' in Arabic.
